decomposition.slice_name: RunResult + stateful executor foundation (single-column path, #193 card-type)
decomposition.intent: Introduce the RunResult object (base + validation-specialized subclass) with its UUID5 identity and normalized/cleaned label, refactor workflow/run.py from free functions into a stateful per-step executor that produces RunResults, and serialize/render them so the existing single-column (N=1) path works end-to-end and the mixed-set wrong card-type bug (#193) is fixed.
decomposition.scope: RunResult type + label normalization util; executor refactor of run.py producing RunResults for the N=1 path; API serialization of RunResults; Results screen renders cards from a list of RunResults (so N results render naturally later). Regression lock on the single-column validation and transform paths.
acceptance: A single-column validation run returns one RunResult carrying status and pass/fail counts; RunResult identity is a deterministic UUID5(function, argument bundle, source).
A single-column transform run returns one RunResult; the existing N=1 transform/validation behavior is unchanged (regression-locked at the workflow seam).
POST /pipelines/{source_id}/run and the validations run endpoint serialize RunResult(s) with a readable label and the UUID5 identity.
The Results screen renders a result card per RunResult from the serialized list, showing pass/fail counts and the label.
A mixed validation/transform set renders the correct card type per result (closes #193 card-type symptom), driven by RunResult function_type.
Label normalization is a pure function: strips leading underscores and odd tokens, never returns an empty label (unit-tested directly).

decomposition.slice_name: Column order: alias_map.position + add-order + minimal reorder control
decomposition.intent: Persist user-placed column order so argument bundles can align by position: add a position column to alias_map, write add-order on attach, read ORDER BY parameter_id, position in the runner and get_pipeline, support reorder via the step PATCH, and ship a minimal conventions-following reorder control in the mapping modal.
decomposition.scope: alias_map.position DDL; attach writes positions in add-order; patch_pipeline_step rewrites positions; run.py/_fetch_steps and get_pipeline order by position (replacing the alphabetical column_name sort); a minimal move-up/down or numbered-order control in the mapping modal in screen-builder.jsx.
acceptance: alias_map has a position INTEGER column; the schema builder creates it.
Attaching a function writes alias_map.position in the order the columns were provided (add-order default).
get_pipeline and the runner read a parameter's bound columns ORDER BY position, not alphabetically by column_name.
The step PATCH endpoint rewrites a parameter's column positions and the new order is read back.
The mapping modal shows a minimal reorder control that changes column order and persists it via the PATCH (frontend test on the rendered control + call).

decomposition.intent: Execute multi_select_eligible validation functions over argument bundles: a pure bundle-pairing function (positional zip, static-param broadcast, equal-length rule), the executor loop producing one RunResult per bundle, scalar-run normalization, the equal-length guard enforced backend and frontend, and multi-column binding in the mapping modal — so a validation function bound to N columns runs N times and surfaces N labeled pass/fail cards.
decomposition.scope: New pure pairing module (workflow/bundles.py); run.py executor loops bundles for validation; scalar-run normalization to a vector; equal-length-among-varying guard in attach (backend) and the mapping modal (frontend); multi-column binding UI in screen-builder.jsx; per-bundle RunResults flow to the Results cards from slice 1.
acceptance: Bundle pairing is a pure function (no DB/worker): zip-by-position yields N ordered bundles; a length-1 static param broadcasts into every bundle; matrix 3,3,1 -> 3 bundles, single-param N -> N bundles, all-static -> 1 bundle (unit-tested directly).

Unequal lengths among varying params (e.g. 3,2) raise a validation error from the pure function and are rejected at attach with a structured failure (not a 500); the frontend mapping modal blocks the mismatched attach.
A validation function bound to N columns produces N RunResults, one per bundle, each labeled by its varying column(s).
A scalar-shaped validation function bound to a column runs once per record and yields a normalized boolean vector (scalar run).
POST run returns the per-bundle RunResults and the Results screen shows N labeled pass/fail cards.
The mapping modal lets a user bind multiple columns to one parameter following existing conventions.

decomposition.slice_name: Multi-select transform execution + output_mode + output-target map
decomposition.intent: Extend the bundle executor to transforms: output_mode append (cleaned auto-label or user-provided name) and replace (overwrite an explicit, user-selected ordered set of target columns paired with bundles) via a new output-target map, write the transformed report, handle the pd.DataFrame whole-table edge, and surface replace-target/append-name selection in the mapping modal.
decomposition.scope: New output-target mapping (DDL in schema/queries.py; writes in attach.py); run.py transform path loops bundles, applies output_mode into the transformed report; append naming (cleaned label/user name); replace into ordered target columns (count must equal bundle count); pd.DataFrame returns whole table; replace-target + append-name UI in screen-builder.jsx.
acceptance: A transform in append mode bound to N columns adds N new columns to the transformed report, each named by a cleaned auto-label or the user-provided name; no collisions.
A transform in replace mode overwrites the N user-selected ordered target columns (bundle i -> target i); the target count must equal the bundle count or the attach is rejected.
The output-target map persists the ordered target columns for a replace step and the runner reads them in order.
A pd.DataFrame transform receives and returns the whole table (one run), regardless of output_mode.
The transformed report reflects the applied transform; each transform run also yields a RunResult.
The mapping modal lets the user choose replace-target columns and an optional append name following existing conventions.

decomposition.slice_name: Results-screen consumption: exports (results report + transformed report) + minimal drawer
decomposition.intent: Surface RunResults for consumption on the Results screen: build the transposed results report (one row per RunResult, pass/fail/metadata, including passing runs, normalized labels) and the transformed report export, and add a minimal results drawer reusing an existing component that shows a RunResult's metadata when a card is opened. Resolves the #193 staging-export-failure symptom.
decomposition.scope: New export module (workflow/export.py) building the transposed results report and the transformed report; export endpoints in api/pipelines.py / api/validations.py; export buttons + a minimal drawer (reusing the existing detail-drawer component from ui.jsx) wired on the Results screen.
acceptance: Exporting a validation run produces a transposed results report: one row per RunResult keyed by its label, with pass/fail (and metadata) columns, including runs that fully passed.
The results report has two entry points: a Functions-page validation run (each attached source ran) and validations tied to a source (each validation function ran).
Exporting a source's transformed report produces its transformed data table after all assigned transforms completed.
All export labels are normalized (no leading underscores or odd tokens) so the file is well-formed.
The staging-export path no longer fails for a mixed validation/transform set (closes the #193 export symptom).
Opening a result card opens a minimal drawer that renders the RunResult's metadata, reusing the existing detail-drawer component rather than a new one.

decomposition.intent: Reconcile the design docs with the frozen execution model: state multi_select_eligible as a granularity-derived label decoupled from current alias_map presence, document the multi-param argument-bundle model with static-param broadcast and the equal-length rule, remove pd.DataFrame from the eligible enumeration (annotate per §12), and introduce the canonical terms scalar run / argument bundle / static param.
decomposition.scope: Edit .claude/design.md and CLAUDE_REFERENCE §12 so they no longer contradict the frozen ledger/CONTEXT.md and ADR-0001.
acceptance: design.md no longer lists pd.DataFrame as multi_select_eligible (it is annotated 'not eligible — full table always passed, see §12').

design.md documents the multi-param argument-bundle positional-pairing model with static-param broadcast and the equal-length-among-varying rule, and uses the canonical terms scalar run / argument bundle / static param.
CLAUDE_REFERENCE §12 is consistent with design.md and CONTEXT.md on multi-select and pd.DataFrame (no remaining contradiction).

decomposition.intent: Replace the minimal reorder control with the polished drag-reorder pane for argument-bundle column order, per the attached visual-design brief. Blocked-on-design; enhances slice 2 and never blocks it.
decomposition.scope: Polished drag-and-drop reorder pane in the Builder, layered on the minimal reorder control (slice 2) and the position backend; requires a design brief before build.
acceptance: The Builder exposes a drag-reorder pane that sets alias_map.position via the existing PATCH and matches the attached design brief.
Reordering via the pane changes the bundle execution order end-to-end (frontend test on the drag interaction + PATCH call).

decomposition.intent: Replace the minimal results drawer with the full rich drawer (full arguments, sources, the check, failing rows), per the attached visual-design brief. Blocked-on-design; enhances the minimal drawer in slice 5 and never blocks it.
decomposition.scope: Polished rich results drawer on the Results screen, layered on the minimal drawer (slice 5) and the RunResult metadata; requires a design brief before build.
acceptance: The result drawer renders the full RunResult detail (arguments, sources, the check, failing rows) matching the attached design brief.
The rich drawer consumes the same RunResult data the minimal drawer used (frontend test on the expanded content).
